title: Dog Swelling Under the Eye: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Noticed swelling under your dog's eye? Learn about tooth abscesses, stings, and why you should consult a vet within 24 hours.
content_html:
TL;DR: Swelling under a dog's eye is most commonly caused by a tooth root abscess and typically requires veterinary attention within 24 hours. Seek immediate emergency care if your dog has difficulty breathing or if the eyeball itself appears abnormal or damaged.

What causes sudden swelling or a lump under my dog's eye?

  • The most common cause is a tooth root abscess, specifically involving the large upper carnassial tooth.
  • Because the roots of these teeth sit directly below the eye socket, an infection can cause a visible bulge or a draining sore on the face.
  • Other common causes include insect stings, blunt force trauma, or localized allergic reactions.

Is swelling under my dog's eye a veterinary emergency?

Urgency Level: Medium.

  • While rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is a sign of significant discomfort and infection that requires care within 24 hours.
  • Seek immediate emergency care if your dog is experiencing difficulty breathing.
  • Seek emergency care if the eyeball itself looks bulging or sunken.
  • Seek emergency care if your dog is pawing at the eye excessively, which can lead to secondary corneal ulcers.

How can a photo of the swelling help my veterinarian triage my dog?

  • Providing a high-quality image allows professionals to see the exact location of the swelling.
  • Photos allow the vet to check for drainage and signs of ocular involvement.
  • Images help differentiate between simple skin issues and deeper dental or structural problems.
  • Visual information ensures your pet gets the right level of priority for their appointment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, swelling under the eye can be attributed to several causes including orbital cellulitis, foreign bodies (e.g., migrating grass awns), zygomatic sialadenitis, orbital hemorrhage, and neoplasia. Orbital cellulitis presents with acute pain on opening the mouth, eyelid swelling, unilateral prolapse of the nictitating membrane, forward displacement of the globe, and conjunctivitis, predominantly in large and hunting breeds. Orbital neoplasms cause exophthalmia, conjunctival and eyelid swelling, strabismus, and exposure keratitis, typically without pain; prognosis is often poor due to the high incidence of malignancy. Acute cases of orbital cellulitis are usually treated with systemic broad-spectrum antibiotics, but drainage is indicated if swelling behind the last molar is present; warm compresses and topical lubricants to protect the cornea are also indicated. Radiographs and ultrasonography of the adjacent teeth, sinuses, and nasal cavity are recommended for further evaluation, especially if relapses occur.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 504)

faqs.question: Could my dog's eye swelling be caused by a bad tooth?
faqs.answer: Yes, a tooth root abscess is one of the most common reasons for swelling in this specific area. The infection travels from the root of the upper tooth up toward the skin below the eye.

faqs.question: Should I apply a warm compress to the swelling?
faqs.answer: A warm compress may provide some temporary relief if the dog allows it, but you must avoid putting pressure on the eye itself. It is not a substitute for veterinary treatment.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human Benadryl for the swelling?
faqs.answer: You should never give your dog medication without consulting a veterinarian first. If the swelling is caused by a tooth infection rather than an allergy, Benadryl will not help and may delay necessary treatment.
